chapter: Chapter 8: Force and Laws of Motion

questions.question: Explain the concept of force with examples.
questions.answer: Force is a push or pull acting on an object that can change its state of motion or shape. Examples include pushing a trolley or pulling a rope.

questions.question: Explain the effects of force on an object.
questions.answer: Force can change the speed of an object, change its direction of motion, start or stop motion, and change the shape of an object.

questions.question: Explain balanced forces with examples.
questions.answer: Balanced forces are equal forces acting in opposite directions that do not change the state of motion of an object.

questions.question: Explain unbalanced forces with examples.
questions.answer: Unbalanced forces are unequal forces acting on an object that cause a change in motion.

questions.question: Explain inertia and its types.
questions.answer: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist change in its state of rest or motion. Types include inertia of rest, motion and direction.

questions.question: Explain Newton’s First Law of Motion.
questions.answer: Newton’s First Law states that an object remains at rest or continues to move with uniform velocity unless acted upon by an unbalanced external force.

questions.question: Explain why Newton’s First Law is called the law of inertia.
questions.answer: Newton’s First Law explains the property of inertia, which is the tendency of objects to resist changes in motion.

questions.question: Explain momentum and write its formula.
questions.answer: Momentum is the quantity of motion possessed by an object and is equal to the product of mass and velocity.

questions.question: Explain the factors affecting momentum.
questions.answer: Momentum depends on the mass and velocity of the object.

questions.question: Explain Newton’s Second Law of Motion.
questions.answer: Newton’s Second Law states that the rate of change of momentum of an object is proportional to the applied force.

questions.question: Explain the formula F = ma.
questions.answer: The formula F = ma states that force equals mass multiplied by acceleration.

questions.question: Explain the SI unit of force.
questions.answer: The SI unit of force is Newton. One Newton is the force required to accelerate a mass of 1 kg by 1 m/s².

questions.question: Explain Newton’s Third Law of Motion.
questions.answer: Newton’s Third Law states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.

questions.question: Explain action and reaction forces with examples.
questions.answer: Action and reaction forces are equal and opposite forces acting on different bodies.

questions.question: Explain rocket propulsion using Newton’s Third Law.
questions.answer: Rockets move upward because gases expelled downward produce an equal and opposite reaction force.

questions.question: Explain friction and its role in motion.
questions.answer: Friction is the force that opposes motion between surfaces in contact.

questions.question: Explain why objects eventually stop moving.
questions.answer: Objects stop moving because friction opposes their motion.

questions.question: Explain inertia of rest with an example.
questions.answer: Inertia of rest is the tendency of an object to remain at rest.

questions.question: Explain inertia of motion with an example.
questions.answer: Inertia of motion is the tendency of a moving object to continue moving.

questions.question: Explain inertia of direction with an example.
questions.answer: Inertia of direction is the tendency of an object to resist change in direction.

questions.question: Explain the importance of Newton’s laws in daily life.
questions.answer: Newton’s laws help explain motion of vehicles, sports activities, and many mechanical systems.

questions.question: Explain why seat belts are important in vehicles.
questions.answer: Seat belts prevent passengers from moving forward due to inertia during sudden stops.

questions.question: Explain why a heavier object has greater inertia.
questions.answer: Heavier objects have greater mass and therefore greater resistance to changes in motion.

questions.question: Explain why momentum increases with velocity.
questions.answer: Momentum depends on velocity, so increasing velocity increases momentum.

questions.question: Explain the contribution of Sir Isaac Newton to physics.
questions.answer: Sir Isaac Newton formulated the three laws of motion which explain the motion of objects.
